---
lightbox: true
---

# Your First App: Adding, Registering, and Understanding `__init__.py`

In this session you will add a pre-built app to your oTree project,
register it in `settings.py`, demo it in the browser, and then open
the code to understand how an oTree app is structured from the inside.
By the end of this part you will be able to read and navigate any
`__init__.py` file and understand what each component does.

## Step 1: Download and add the `demographic_survey` app

The example code for this course is stored in a shared Google Drive
folder. Navigate to the link below and download the file called
`demographic_survey.zip`:

[Example Code — Google Drive](https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/1BvU8I4FX5KS2RZga_O4Pi-s1PAhVAz-C)

Once downloaded:

1. Locate `demographic_survey.zip` in your Downloads folder
2. Double-click the file to unzip it — this will produce a folder
called `demographic_survey`
3. Move the `demographic_survey` folder into your oTree project
folder — for example, into `oTree_lab`

After moving it, open VS Code and check the Explorer panel. You
should now see `demographic_survey` listed alongside the other app
folders inside your project.

::: {.callout-warning}
## The folder must be inside the project folder
The `demographic_survey` folder must sit directly inside your oTree
project folder — at the same level as `settings.py` and the other
app folders. If it is placed anywhere else, oTree will not be able
to find it.
:::

## Step 2: Register the app in `SESSION_CONFIGS`

Adding the folder to your project is not enough on its own. oTree
only runs apps that are listed in `SESSION_CONFIGS` inside
`settings.py`. Open `settings.py` and add the following dictionary
to your `SESSION_CONFIGS` list:

```python
SESSION_CONFIGS = [
    # ... your existing session dictionaries ...

    dict(
        name='survey_leveled_up',
        display_name="End of experiment questionnaires",
        app_sequence=['demographic_survey'],
        num_demo_participants=1,
        doc="Standard end of experiment questionnaires."
    ),
]
```

Notice the five key-value pairs in this dictionary:

| Key | Value | What it does |
|-----|-------|-------------|
| `name` | `'survey_leveled_up'` | Internal identifier used by the server |
| `display_name` | `"End of experiment questionnaires"` | The name shown in the admin interface |
| `app_sequence` | `['demographic_survey']` | The list of apps in this session — just one here |
| `num_demo_participants` | `1` | One participant is enough to demo a survey |
| `doc` | `"Standard end of experiment questionnaires."` | A short note describing the session — optional but good practice |

Save `settings.py` after making the change.

::: {.callout-important}
## Reset the database after editing `settings.py`
Any time you edit `settings.py` — including adding a new session —
you must reset the database before running the server. In the
terminal, make sure you are inside your project folder, then run:

```bash
otree resetdb
```

Confirm with `y` when prompted.
:::

## Step 3: Demo the app

With the database reset, start the development server:

```bash
otree devserver
```

Open your browser and go to `http://localhost:8000/demo`. You should
now see `End of experiment questionnaires` listed on the Demo page.
Click on it to launch a demo session and work through the app as a
participant would. When you are done, press **Ctrl + C** in the
terminal to stop the server.

## Step 4: Open `__init__.py` and understand the structure

Every oTree app has exactly one `__init__.py` file. This single
Python file contains everything the app needs to run: the constants,
the data models, the page logic, and the page sequence. Open
`demographic_survey/__init__.py` in VS Code. Here is the full file:

```python
from otree.api import *

doc = """
Standard, end-of-session survey.
Includes Cognitive Reflection Task and demographic survey.
"""

class C(BaseConstants):
    NAME_IN_URL = 'survey_experiment'
    PLAYERS_PER_GROUP = None
    NUM_ROUNDS = 1


class Subsession(BaseSubsession):
    pass


class Group(BaseGroup):
    pass


class Player(BasePlayer):
    age = models.IntegerField(
        label='What is your age?',
        min=18,
        max=99,
    )
    gender = models.StringField(
        choices=[['Male', 'Male'], ['Female', 'Female'],
                 ['Non-Binary', 'Non-Binary'],
                 ['NoState', 'Prefer not to state']],
        label='What is your gender?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    race = models.StringField(
        choices=[
            ['Black', 'African descent, Black, African-American'],
            ['Asian', 'Asian descent, Asian-American'],
            ['Indigenous', 'Indigenous, First Nations, Native American'],
            ['MiddleEastern', 'Middle Eastern or North African'],
            ['Hawaiian', 'Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander'],
            ['White', 'White'],
            ['Other', 'Other'],
            ['Declined', 'Decline to state']
        ],
        label='What is your race?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    ethnicity = models.BooleanField(
        choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
        label='Are you of Latinx/o/a or Hispanic origin?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    school = models.StringField(
        choices=[
            ['Social', 'School of Social Sciences'],
            ['Ecology', 'School of Social Ecology'],
            ['Law', 'School of Law'],
            ['Humanities', 'School of Humanities'],
            ['Education', 'School of Education'],
            ['Arts', 'Claire Trevor School of the Arts'],
            ['Business', 'The Paul Merage School of Business'],
            ['Biological', 'School of Biological Sciences'],
            ['Engineering', 'The Henry Samueli School of Engineering'],
            ['Computers', 'Donald Bren School of Information and Computer Sciences'],
            ['Interdisciplinary', 'Interdisciplinary Studies'],
            ['Physical', 'School of Physical Sciences'],
            ['Medicine', 'School of Medicine'],
            ['Nursing', 'Sue and Bill Gross School of Nursing'],
            ['Pharmaceutical', 'School of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences'],
            ['Health', 'Program in Public Health']
        ],
        label='Choose the most appropriate option for your school below.',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    field_of_study = models.StringField(
        label='What is your major or field of study?',
    )
    major_STEM = models.BooleanField(
        choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
        label='Are you majoring in a STEM field?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    math_courses = models.IntegerField(
        label='How many Mathematics/Statistics courses have you taken since High School?',
        min=0,
        max=99,
    )
    english_is_first_language = models.BooleanField(
        choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
        label='Is English your first language?',
        widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
    )
    first_language = models.StringField(
        label='Please state your first language.',
        blank=True,
    )

    # ~~~ Cognitive Reflection Task

    crt_q1 = models.StringField(
        label="If John can drink one barrel of water in 6 days, and Mary can drink one barrel of water in 12 days, how many days would it take them to drink one barrel of water together?"
    )
    crt_q2 = models.StringField(
        label="A man buys a pig for \$60, sells it for \$70, buys it back for \$80, and sells it finally for \$90. How much money has he made in dollars?"
    )
    crt_q3 = models.StringField(
        label="Jerry received both the 15th highest and the 15th lowest mark in the class. How many students are in the class?"
    )


# ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ PAGES

class Questionnaire_CRT(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['crt_q1', 'crt_q2', 'crt_q3']

    @staticmethod
    def is_displayed(player):
        return player.round_number == C.NUM_ROUNDS


class Demographics(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['age', 'gender', 'race', 'ethnicity', 'school',
                   'field_of_study', 'major_STEM', 'math_courses',
                   'english_is_first_language', 'first_language']


page_sequence = [Questionnaire_CRT, Demographics]
```

Now let us go through every part of this file.

---

### The import statement

```python
from otree.api import *
```

This line is the same in every single oTree app. It imports all of
oTree's built-in classes, field types, widgets, and functions. You
will never change this line.

---

### The `doc` string

```python
doc = """
Standard, end-of-session survey.
Includes Cognitive Reflection Task and demographic survey.
"""
```

The `doc` variable is an optional description of the app. It appears
in the admin interface when you click on the app sequence for a
session. It has no effect on how the app runs — it is purely
informational. Write something useful here so that anyone looking at
the admin interface understands what this app does without having to
open the code.

---

### Class `C`: Constants

```python
class C(BaseConstants):
    NAME_IN_URL = 'survey_experiment'
    PLAYERS_PER_GROUP = None
    NUM_ROUNDS = 1
```

The `C` class holds all the fixed parameters of your app — values
that are set before the session starts and do not change while it
runs.

| Constant | Value | What it means |
|----------|-------|--------------|
| `NAME_IN_URL` | `'survey_experiment'` | The string that appears in the participant's browser URL during the experiment |
| `PLAYERS_PER_GROUP` | `None` | Participants are not grouped — this is an individual task |
| `NUM_ROUNDS` | `1` | Participants go through the page sequence once |

::: {.callout-warning}
## Participants can see `NAME_IN_URL`
Whatever string you assign to `NAME_IN_URL` will be visible in the
browser address bar during the experiment. Notice that this app uses
`'survey_experiment'` rather than `'demographic_survey'` — this is
deliberate. The name does not reveal what kind of survey is being
administered. Do not use names that hint at the purpose or design
of your study.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Python naming conventions
Constant variables like `NAME_IN_URL` are written in
`UPPERCASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES`. Non-constant variables and function
names use `lowercase_with_underscores`. Class names use `PascalCase`
(e.g., `BasePlayer`, `WaitPage`). VS Code's colour-coding works
better when you follow these conventions.
:::

---

### Data model classes: `Subsession`, `Group`, and `Player`

Every `__init__.py` must define three data model classes. These
represent the three levels of oTree's data structure and correspond
directly to tables in the database.

```python
class Subsession(BaseSubsession):
    pass

class Group(BaseGroup):
    pass
```

Both `Subsession` and `Group` contain only `pass` here because this
app collects individual participant data only. No group-level or
round-level variables are needed.

::: {.callout-note}
## What `pass` means
In Python, `pass` is a placeholder that means "this class exists but
has nothing extra defined in it." oTree requires all three model
classes to be present in every `__init__.py`. If you are not adding
any fields or methods to `Subsession` or `Group`, you must still
include the class definition with `pass` underneath it. Removing
either class entirely will crash the app.
:::

---

### The `Player` class and model fields

The `Player` class is where all the participant-level data lives.
Every field you define here becomes a column in your exported data.

#### Field types used in this app

| Field type | Used for | Example in this app |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| `models.IntegerField()` | Whole numbers | `age`, `math_courses` |
| `models.StringField()` | Text or multiple choice | `gender`, `race`, `school`, `field_of_study` |
| `models.BooleanField()` | True/False answers | `ethnicity`, `major_STEM`, `english_is_first_language` |

#### Field arguments used in this app

| Argument | What it does |
|----------|-------------|
| `label` | The question or prompt shown to the participant on the page |
| `min` / `max` | Sets the minimum and maximum values for numeric fields |
| `choices` | A list of options for multiple choice questions |
| `widget` | Controls how the input is displayed — `widgets.RadioSelect` shows radio buttons |
| `blank` | If `True`, the field is optional and participants can skip it |

#### How `choices` works

This is one of the most important things to understand about oTree
model fields. The `choices` argument takes a list of two-item lists,
where each inner list follows this pattern:

```python
choices = [[recorded_in_database, shown_to_participant], ...]
```

Look at the `gender` field:

```python
gender = models.StringField(
    choices=[['Male', 'Male'], ['Female', 'Female'],
             ['Non-Binary', 'Non-Binary'],
             ['NoState', 'Prefer not to state']],
    label='What is your gender?',
    widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
)
```

The participant sees four options on screen: Male, Female, Non-Binary,
and Prefer not to state. But notice that `'NoState'` is what gets
recorded in the database — not `'Prefer not to state'`. The first
value in each pair is what oTree saves; the second is what the
participant reads.

Now look at the `ethnicity` field:

```python
ethnicity = models.BooleanField(
    choices=[[True, 'Yes'], [False, 'No']],
    label='Are you of Latinx/o/a or Hispanic origin?',
    widget=widgets.RadioSelect,
)
```

The participant sees Yes or No. The database records `True` or
`False`. This is a clean and efficient way to store binary responses
— it makes analysis much easier than storing the strings "Yes" and
"No" directly.

::: {.callout-important}
## The `choices` format is always `[recorded, displayed]`
The first value in each pair is stored in the database. The second
value is what the participant sees. Never mix these up. If you do,
your data will contain the wrong values and you will not catch the
error until analysis.
:::

#### The `blank=True` argument

```python
first_language = models.StringField(
    label='Please state your first language.',
    blank=True,
)
```

By default, all form fields in oTree are required — participants
cannot proceed to the next page without filling them in. Setting
`blank=True` makes a field optional. Here `first_language` is
optional because only participants who answered No to
`english_is_first_language` need to complete it.

#### Section comments

```python
# ~~~ Cognitive Reflection Task
```

This is just a Python comment — a line beginning with `#` that oTree
ignores entirely. It is there purely for readability, separating the
demographic fields from the CRT fields. Use comments freely in your
own code to mark where one section ends and another begins.

---

### Page classes

Each page in your app is defined as a class that inherits from
`Page`. This app has two pages.

#### `Questionnaire_CRT`

```python
class Questionnaire_CRT(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['crt_q1', 'crt_q2', 'crt_q3']

    @staticmethod
    def is_displayed(player):
        return player.round_number == C.NUM_ROUNDS
```

| Attribute or method | What it does |
|--------------------|-------------|
| `form_model = 'player'` | Tells oTree the form fields belong to the `Player` model |
| `form_fields` | Lists the three CRT fields that will appear as inputs on this page |
| `@staticmethod` | Required decorator before any Page-level method when working in VS Code or any IDE |
| `is_displayed` | A built-in oTree method that controls whether this page is shown to a participant. It returns `True` or `False`. |

The `is_displayed` method here returns:

```python
return player.round_number == C.NUM_ROUNDS
```

Since `C.NUM_ROUNDS = 1`, this evaluates to `player.round_number == 1`,
which is always `True` for a single-round app. In a multi-round app,
this would restrict the page to only the final round. For now it is
effectively always displayed.

::: {.callout-tip}
## `@staticmethod` is required for Page methods in VS Code
When you define any built-in oTree method inside a Page class —
such as `is_displayed`, `vars_for_template`, or `before_next_page`
— you must precede it with `@staticmethod`. This is because these
methods are defined at the Page level rather than outside it. If
you forget `@staticmethod`, the method will either not work or
will produce an error.
:::

#### `Demographics`

```python
class Demographics(Page):
    form_model = 'player'
    form_fields = ['age', 'gender', 'race', 'ethnicity', 'school',
                   'field_of_study', 'major_STEM', 'math_courses',
                   'english_is_first_language', 'first_language']
```

This page has no special methods — just `form_model` and
`form_fields`. All ten demographic fields will appear on this page
in the order they are listed in `form_fields`. There is no
`is_displayed` method, so this page is always shown.

---

### The `page_sequence` list

```python
page_sequence = [Questionnaire_CRT, Demographics]
```

This list controls the order in which participants see the pages.
Participants will complete the CRT first, then the demographics
questionnaire. If you swap the order here, they will see the
demographics first.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Every page must be in `page_sequence`
If you define a Page class but forget to add it to `page_sequence`,
oTree will not show it to participants. Conversely, if you list a
page in `page_sequence` that does not have a corresponding class
definition, the app will crash when you try to run it.
:::

---

## Summary: The anatomy of `__init__.py`

| Section | What it contains | Required? |
|---------|-----------------|-----------| 
| `from otree.api import *` | The oTree import statement | Always |
| `doc` | A short description of the app | Optional but recommended |
| `class C(BaseConstants)` | `NAME_IN_URL`, `PLAYERS_PER_GROUP`, `NUM_ROUNDS` | Always |
| `class Subsession` | Round-level logic and fields | Always — use `pass` if empty |
| `class Group` | Group-level logic and fields | Always — use `pass` if empty |
| `class Player` | Individual participant fields | Always — add your data fields here |
| Page classes | One class per page, with `form_model`, `form_fields`, and any methods | Always — at least one |
| `page_sequence` | The ordered list of page classes | Always |
